review_text:
I love this app. I’ve been using it for years to learn Spanish. The lessons are clear and informative. 

I just don’t like how the app is trying to become one big game nowadays. Between streaks, leagues, quests, etc- I find that there are too many distractions that pop up, when all I want to do is move on to the next lesson. I feel like it’s wasting my time.
I like to learn multiple times a week, but not every single day. When a new streak starts, then gets lost, again and again, it frustrates me. I don’t care for a streak, I just want to learn. 

I’d love if there could be a setting where you can decide if you want game style vs. focused work. 
Please can you do that for us focused learners? 
Thank you! :)

The changes they made do not help with continued learning. I have used this app for a few years, but now it is different. The update they implemented for the free version—limiting users to just 25 points per lesson—hinders the learning process, as those points are almost entirely consumed by the time you finish a single lesson. You then have to replenish them via the "free chest," which consists of nothing but ads—ad after ad after ad. Each ad grants you 5 points, meaning you have to watch 5 separate commercials, which is time-consuming. Furthermore, upon completing a lesson, they repeatedly push for the purchase of Super Duolingo—which seems to be their sole objective now—over and over again. It has become difficult to keep learning, and quite annoying. I don't think they will ever revert this new update regarding the 25-point-per-lesson limit. They ruined this app.

Sometimes when Duolingo updates or adds new sections, it’ll complete sections or move around sections that you’ve never done. So say I’m on unit 2 section 7, and it reshuffles the units, units that used to be after that are now before will be completed. So moving forward, it thinks you’ve already learned those sections, and you didn’t. So it’s as if you skipped several sections and units, which makes moving forward a lot harder because it’ll have a bunch of vocab that you’ve never learned.

Duolingo used to be super accessible, but now all but required you to pay for premium. You get MAYBE 5-7 minutes every 24 hours of free use before running out of "charge" (an in-app metric which is depleted both by time active and every question answered). After every 2 minute lesson you are given an ad, so of the 7 minutes you're allowed to use the app you'll receive 3 or more ads.

There are some super cool features the app offers, but are rendered entirely useless by the pay wall. For example, I enabled it to block access to other apps before completing a lesson, but if I don't have any charge the next day then I just can't access my phone without buying gems to refill the in-app charge. It's crazy!

I remember back in the day duolingo was a free program with no ads that wasn't constantly trying to scam you into giving them money. Its honestly a major bummer to see the way it's shilled out. I guess nothing good lasts forever before corporate interests take ahold!

I highly recommend looking elsewhere for an actually free platform. If you use duolingo you're just going to end up spending $100 a year for basic features.

Would give 0/5 if I could and solely because the “Flashcard Frenzy” mode is by far the worst feature I have ever had the displeasure of using. Everything else is fine but this one feature—which the developers integrate into overall progress—is abysmal. 

It is a good idea in theory, but horrendous and thoughtless in execution. There is no way to distinguish between multiple translations of the same word, so one must guess all possible answers every time or give up and retry. 

That issue is minuscule compared to the more glaring problem, which is the atrocious “voice recognition.” God himself could come down and shout the word, and half the time the application would not recognize any sound. Of those times it does recognize sound, the bar for “correct” is vexingly unclear. Audio and voice recognition work fine for every other speaking feature in the app, so why the developers are unable to make it even mildly passable for Flashcard Frenzy is truly a mystery.

review_title: Premium has ADS to upsell gems
review_text:
UPDATE:
As annoying as ever. Potential buyers should understand this app intentionally changes the positions of the ‘buy power ups’ and ‘decline’ buttons from left to right, so that you never know where to press to dismiss an offer of purchasing points to continue a round.  This is incredibly annoying, inefficient for the player, and is in bad faith.  It leaves a bad taste in my mouth that will be remembered when it comes time to renew. 

I bought the “no ad” family plan… yet Duolingo wants to hawk their gem packs for 99 cents, 4.99 or 9.99 when I haven’t played as much, so I don’t have more gems earned/ stored . I should be able to shut off this nonsense in settings. It makes sense to allow you to power up IF and only if you have enough gems collected from play, but having in-app purchases in an already PAID app is predatory. A PAID app should NEVER have in-app purchases pushed at someone, unless they actively choose to have them shown to them in the settings. Shame on you all for doing this in PAID plans. PARENTS beware of this predatory feature and make sure in-app purchases are set to OFF on your child’s device- they will still be bothered and nagged on their device/their time will be wasted dealing with these ridiculous popups, but at least you won’t have unexpected charges for what you thought was a “PREMIUM” all inclusive subscription. This is why I generally don’t buy subscriptions… exactly because of nonsense like this!

The app is most focused on gamification. 
Unfortunately it does not do a good job teaching basic grammar rules and doesn’t differentiate when it’s ‚they‘ or ‚formal you‘ (in Spanish) so it‘ll show ‚you‘ but when you say ‚tu‘ it’s ‚ustedes‘ instead or vice versa. You have to guess the grammar structure as Duolingo does not show how to conjugate words, at max two versions instead of all (I, he,she,it, they etc) and it’s a guessing game with verb stems being different rules. (Now I signed up for another language app to learn what Duolingo is lacking). 
At time it will tell you one word e.g. mountain as ‚Montana‘ but on the next lesson it’s ‚el monte‘ or maybe I am just confused now as there is not enough explanation of why/how. And especially no or poor explanation when you use the wrong word e.g ‚hay‘ instead of ‚esta‘ - again I have to look elsewhere to figure my mistake. 
Very frustrating at this point as I am now using two apps.

review_title: Flashcard Frenzy sucks; please bring back Match Madness
review_text:
Please get rid of Flashcard Frenzy as the main game on the leaderboard. 

Your audio detection technology is not nearly accurate enough yet. It wouldn’t even consistently recognize my French girlfriend speaking French. 

Besides that, Match Madness is one of the most important aspect of Duolingo for me personally because it helps me get repetition for learning words. The “practice” one just isn’t the same. 

Please, please get rid of this “limited time event” like you promised (Flashcard). Please bring back Match Madness. 

Thank you ♥️

ps you have been shipping way too many updates lately. The app is breaking in a lot of small ways for me and my friends. Please focus on this core foundational functionality first. It is much more important.

review_title: This used to be an amazing app!
review_text:
Been using this app for over 3 years and I loved it. I was making strides in learning Spanish.

My experience has been short (185 day streak), but long enough to see the steady decline of the app. I’ve never paid for this app and have only had the regular (aside from being randomly gifted super for periods), and I’ve noticed the free version slowly getting worse. They mess with the “energy” system like, once or twice a month, and it never seems to settle into any sort of groove. This most recent update has taken away any and all ability to do long periods of learning on the app — an extremely obvious way to convince consumers to use the paid version. Energy recharges less, ads recharge less, and you can, AT MOST, get 3 full lessons out of this current system before needing to watch a swarm of ads or spend gems which are so sparingly doled out. Additionally, the AI is full of inaccuracies and inconsistencies that make learning things frustrating when you can TELL they don’t make sense. I also can’t stand when the app updates and it randomly changes its lesson plans, which then treats old words like brand new ones and throws new words in at random as if they’ve been a part of your lessons already. When it works, it works great. But just like many companies, its over-reliance on AI comes at the expense of the user, and they have to turn towards cheap gimmicks like making the app harder to use for free and paying for skins like it’s Fortnite to make money. It’s been tough to watch it get worse, and I’ve stuck through it because learning this language is valuable to me, but my long-term confidence in this app maintaining any sort of quality standard is INCREDIBLY low.

First, I tested. I liked the general process so I purchased so I could go ad free. It isn’t. The ads are just reduced. Constantly trying to get me to upgrade. If I wanted to or had use to, I would. Don’t lie, though, and say it’s ad free when it isn’t. 

Secondly, the way the lessons are structured and the way you progress is good. Solid. Highly repetitive as you advance while also learning new. Solid from that perspective. 

Third, and admittedly this is a bit of an ego thing, but the new vocabulary and concepts just jump right in to testing. Lowering your score for something that, by all accounts, you shouldn’t know when you invariably get it wrong. Then it jumps straight into repetition and helping to learn. 

Fourth, the font size is, at times, very difficult to see. No iPad adjustment has been able to fix. It’s worse on phone as it just compresses the screen that much more. The fonts are often thin, not dark, and small making it very difficult to see clearly. Especially when small differences in characters make the difference between one character and another. 

Lastly, in practice, the app is buggy, at times unresponsive, slow, crashes, and the like. And did I mention ads on the purchased version? Yeah, they don’t go away. Parts of lessons and steps are only available for top tier. Why? I know why but I’ve already given enough money, stop dangling better teachings for more money (aka: ads!). 

I already paid for the year. So I’ll keep it. But at this point unlikely to renew given the issues stated above.

Yeah, it’s a really functional app. 
But apps live and die in the long run on the content they provide. For language content you really gotta be careful on which topics you cover and how functionally needed Those topics are gonna be. I sure that you guys probably have professionals, actively researching what parts of language to teach. But I’m learning Korean, and I’ve spent how many lessons learning specifically the numbers 5, 19 and 30. I’ve gone to ChatGPT to find out all the numbers in between because there doesn’t seem to be a 1 through 10 lesson anywhere. And the number of lessons where I need to memorize how to spell the word cat. I don’t do more than one lesson these days because I am so annoyed by the content in each lesson.

I’m honestly just doing this to keep my knowledge Hangeul alive, when I actually decide to travel to Korea, I’m headed for Rosetta Stone and I’m quitting this app. Maybe then I’ll learn and ask where the bathroom is instead of somebody’s age… lol

It would have been nice to have gotten a renewal notification. I get notifications for every other minuscule thing about people I don’t even know but not notification for my renewal. Then there’s no option for refund. For an app to be about improving communication you’re sugar honey ice tea at it.

Besides the slow to roll lessons and narrow range of topics which are more games than learning, my iphone mini now crashes almost daily. The app is clearly not optimized as it makes my phone so hot it is hard to hold. Complains and reports by email are answered by AI which just tells you to give more detail, even when that detail was included in the report. Lessons are designed to be frustrating hoping for engagement until you fix what's wrong (some sentences are so rigid and unnatural, you need to memorize the answers in English to pass to the next question). Many lessons already completed (gold) revert to not complete. Despite a 2300 day streak, I'm still barely improving my oral communication skills, only able to understand written test in the correct context.

I spent almost a year trying to speak Spanish with this app and even with multiple native speakers all I’ve gotten is made fun of. No matter what type of language you learn on here it’s wrong, save yourself the embarrassment and don’t even try this garbage
Best language app ever made.

This could be good supplementally but it does not teach you the language.  It won’t explain grammar rules and you’ll come across little quirks in the language that it won’t explain well. 

Add onto that a game system that seems to set you up for failure and all you end up with is a pretty good exercise in frustration. 

This was made even worse when they updated the Japanese path recently but didn’t tell me what concertos got moved adding, so I just come across concepts and words that it expects me to know but I’ve never seen before. 

Also, recently they updated the energy system so if you are planning on doing this for free and want to just finish the daily quests it gives you, you now have to watch 3 times as many commercials.

review_title: Reasoning for four versus five stars
review_text:
The Spanish isn’t always accurate. While I am not a native Spanish speaker and very white, both born adopted, and raised, my Bachelor of Arts degree is in Spanish. I feel confident that I could find at minimum five native speakers, who would agree with me.

I also feel that the content is very pro-military versus pro-people, and that’s absolutely absurd…

Please reevaluate what you’ll eventually be teaching my kid.

Thank you for understanding my concern regarding this matter.

—Zenith fae Gracyon.
